title: Agencies request comment on proposals to modernize the regulatory capital framework and maintain the strength of the banking system
summary: The FDIC, Federal Reserve Board, and OCC jointly requested comment on three proposals to modernize the regulatory capital framework for banks of all sizes, aiming to streamline capital requirements and better align regulatory capital with risk while maintaining safety and soundness. The first proposal would primarily apply to the largest internationally active banks and would implement the final components of the Basel III agreement. The second proposal targets smaller banks, better aligning capital requirements for traditional lending activities. The third proposal, from the Fed only, would improve systemic risk measurement for determining additional capital requirements for the largest and most complex banks. Comment deadline is June 18, 2026.
key_points: Three separate capital framework modernization proposals released jointly by FDIC, Fed, and OCC on March 19, 2026
First proposal (Category I/II banks): would streamline framework to one set of risk-based capital calculations instead of two, improving risk sensitivity and implementing final Basel III components
Second proposal (smaller banks): would better align capital requirements for traditional lending activities while maintaining framework simplicity; includes mortgage servicing modifications
Third proposal (Fed-only): would improve systemic risk measurement for determining G-SIB capital surcharges (FR Y-15)
Large banks would be required to reflect unrealized gains and losses on certain securities in regulatory capital levels, subject to transition period
Agencies anticipate modest overall capital decrease in the banking system, but levels would remain substantially higher than pre-financial crisis
why_it_matters: These proposals represent a significant recalibration of bank capital regulation. The direction toward reduced capital requirements for both large and smaller banks could affect bank profitability, lending capacity, and systemic risk buffers. The inclusion of unrealized securities gains/losses in capital calculations for large banks could increase volatility in regulatory capital ratios. Changes to G-SIB surcharges and systemic risk measurement could alter competitive dynamics among the largest banks.
market_structure_context: The proposals would modify the foundational regulatory capital framework governing how U.S. banks calculate and maintain capital requirements. Changes include: (1) consolidation of risk-based capital calculation methods for large banks; (2) improved calibration of credit, market, and operational risk capture; (3) modifications to mortgage servicing asset capital treatment; (4) changes to systemic risk measurement methodology (FR Y-15) for G-SIBs. These are foundational changes to bank prudential regulation that would affect how capital markets price bank risk.
notes: Source is a high-quality official joint press release from three federal banking regulators. Document quality is good with clear structure. Three linked Notices of Proposed Rulemaking are referenced (risk-based capital surcharges for G-SIBs; Category I/II Banking Organizations; Standardized Approach for RWA) but only summaries are included - the actual NPRs would contain detailed rule text. Supporting documents include aggregated data release, fact sheet, and statements from Chair Powell and other Fed officials. The source is recent (March 2026) and directly relevant to market structure via banking regulation. No speculative market impact language present.
